items.title: Thermostat control without labeled actions
items.scenario: A smart thermostat adjusts heating every 15 minutes. It does not receive a label saying "set to 68°F now." Instead it gets a reward based on comfort and energy cost after each interval. Over weeks it learns a policy that keeps occupants comfortable while minimizing bills.
items.explanation: This is RL because feedback is a reward signal tied to outcomes, actions affect future states, and the best policy emerges from cumulative experience—not from a fixed dataset of (state, correct action) pairs.

items.title: Warehouse robot navigation
items.scenario: A mobile robot in a warehouse picks actions (move forward, turn, stop) from camera and lidar observations. It receives +1 for delivering a package on time and −1 for collisions. Early random moves cause bumps; later the policy routes around obstacles efficiently.
items.explanation: The agent, environment, state (sensor readings), actions, and rewards are explicit. Sparse collision penalties make credit assignment hard—the robot must learn which earlier turns led to safe paths.

items.title: Game playing (AlphaGo-style)
items.scenario: A board-game agent selects moves from a position (state). It wins (+1) or loses (−1) only at the end of the game, thousands of steps after many individual moves. Self-play generates data to improve the policy and value estimates.
items.explanation: Delayed terminal rewards are typical in RL. The policy must optimize long-term return, not myopic local gains—connecting to value functions and Monte Carlo / temporal-difference learning.

items.title: RLHF for a chat assistant (Gen AI bridge)
items.scenario: After supervised fine-tuning, a language model generates replies (actions) given prompts (states). A reward model scores helpfulness and safety. Policy optimization nudges the model toward higher-scoring responses without human labels for every token.
items.explanation: RLHF reuses the RL paradigm: the LLM is the agent, prompts are states, generated text is the action sequence, and learned preference scores are rewards—linking classical RL vocabulary to modern alignment pipelines.
